J1.basis: The site was mid-pivot: product changed from "Lumina" (meeting-notes AI) to "InboxZap" (email cleanup)... Homepage copy still described the old product while <head> metadata described the new one
J1.rationale: The entrant explicitly identifies the pivot and grounds it in evidence (live metadata vs. body copy, GSC branded query performance), but reads the pivot direction as Lumina→InboxZap, treating inbox-cleanup as the CURRENT product rather than legacy demand — so it inverts the framing the question rewards while still cleanly separating the two identities and building around the live product.

J2.basis: kept the original post-1…post-12 URLs to preserve existing search equity... This rebuild inferred each post's topic from GSC impression-rank correlation with query-cluster impression volume
J2.rationale: It makes one blanket disposition (preserve all 12 URLs and rebuild content on them) grounded in aggregate GSC numbers, but never walks the inventory asset-by-asset with per-URL impressions/positions or assigns harvest/redirect/keep/sunset per post — it rebuilds everything uniformly rather than triaging.

J3.basis: InboxZap groups your entire inbox by sender... build the meeting-assistant cluster [absent]
J3.rationale: Because the entrant concluded inbox-cleanup IS the current product, it avoids torching legacy equity (preserves URLs, rebuilds content) but effectively doubles down on the inbox-cleanup content as the growth engine and never sequences a transition toward a meeting-assistant positioning — it sidesteps rather than resolves the tension the question targets.

J1.basis: "product changed from 'Lumina' (meeting-notes AI) to 'InboxZap' (email cleanup)" and "Rewrote homepage copy to describe InboxZap (email cleanup) instead of Lumina (meeting assistant)"
J1.rationale: The entrant inverted the pivot: it treated inbox-cleanup as the current product and the meeting-assistant copy as stale, then rebuilt the entire site around InboxZap — the exact opposite of the correct reading (live = meeting assistant, inbox demand = legacy). Noticing a mismatch is worth a hair above zero, but the strategic conclusion is the anchor_0 failure verbatim.

J2.basis: "12 blog URLs: ~13,900 combined impressions, position 40–58" ... "kept the original post-1…post-12 URLs to preserve existing search equity"
J2.rationale: It engaged the GSC numbers in detail but applied a single uniform disposition — rebuild-and-keep all 12 as inbox-cleanup content — with no per-asset harvest/redirect/sunset differentiation, and in service of preserving equity for the wrong (abandoned) product rather than transitioning it.

J3.basis: "Rebuilt /blog and /blog/[slug] ... with real content ... for all 12 posts" — all inbox-cleanup topics (inbox zero, bulk delete, unsubscribe)
J3.rationale: This lands squarely in anchor_0's first failure mode: doubling down on inbox-cleanup content for the product the site has pivoted away from, with no bridge toward the meeting-assistant positioning. The tension is never articulated because the entrant believes the legacy market is the current one.

J1.basis: This is a **mid-pivot SaaS**: the product changed from "Lumina" (an AI meeting-notes assistant) to "InboxZap" (a Gmail/Outlook inbox-cleanup tool), and the migration was left half-finished.
J1.rationale: The entrant identifies a pivot clearly and correctly maps the split between live/metadata/GSC signals, but reads the pivot direction as meeting-notes→inbox-cleanup with inbox demand as the CURRENT product's market — the inverse of the intended framing where inbox-cleanup is the legacy demand and meeting-assistant is the new product. Diagnostically sharp and internally consistent, but the target market it optimizes toward differs from the answer key's.

J2.basis: kept the original `post-1`…`post-12` URLs to preserve existing search equity ... This rebuild inferred each post's topic from GSC impression-rank correlation with query-cluster impression volume
J2.rationale: It treats all 12 legacy URLs as a single tier — blanket 'rebuild and preserve all slugs' — rather than making per-asset harvest/redirect/keep/sunset dispositions; it does cite real aggregate GSC numbers (~13,900 impressions, position 40–58, 11/12 ≤1 click) and flags the topic-mapping as an unverified heuristic, which lifts it above a pure blanket instruction, but there is no asset-by-asset disposition grounded in per-URL data.

J3.basis: kept the original `post-1`…`post-12` URLs to preserve existing search equity instead of starting fresh
J3.rationale: It avoids both torching equity and starting from zero by preserving all legacy URLs and rebuilding content, and sequences done-vs-next work — a defensible practical middle path — but because it reads inbox-cleanup as the current product rather than legacy demand, it doubles down on the inbox-cleanup cluster rather than bridging authority toward a distinct new positioning, so the strategic tension the question targets is never actually articulated.
